description: The image displays a software user-interface element labeled 'Check for updates' featuring a red bookmark symbol inside a circular badge, meaning the intended scientific figure is completely unreadable. No sub-panels, curves, spectra, microstructural regions, data axes, or scale bars are visible. Based solely on the provided paper context for the low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B), the study investigates a triplex microstructure containing austenite, δ-ferrite, and in-situ TiB2 particles, reporting a density of 6.88 g cm^-3, a Young's modulus of 225 GPa, a yield strength of 503 MPa, and a uniform elongation of 13%. The work utilizes SEM, EBSD, TEM, synchrotron XRD, micro-CT, μ-DIC, and finite element simulations to examine deformation mechanisms and damage evolution, but none of these scientific contents can be visually confirmed from the current image.

Figure 1 presents the alloy design and processing routes for the low-density steel. Panel (a) plots equilibrium volume fractions of Liquid (red), Ferrite (gray), Austenite (blue), and TiB₂ (gold) versus temperature from phase diagram calculations. TiB₂ remains near 0.15 from 600 °C to ~1400 °C. Austenite dominates below ~950 °C at ~0.85, then decreases with increasing temperature, while ferrite emerges and peaks near 0.85 around 1200 °C, producing a three-phase ferrite-austenite-TiB₂ region between ~950 °C and ~1150 °C. Liquid appears above ~1350 °C and reaches a volume fraction of 1.0 near 1450 °C. Panel (b) schematically illustrates the processing sequence from induction melting and casting through forging/homogenization to hot rolling; the caption states that the 50 kg ingot was homogenized at 1373 K for 2 h and hot-rolled in the two-phase region with a finishing temperature of 1173 K to a 3 mm sheet, followed by air cooling. These panels collectively establish that the alloy composition and processing window were designed using the phase stability predictions to obtain a triplex microstructure comprising stable TiB₂ particles in a dual-phase matrix.

Figure 2 illustrates the finite element simulation set-ups for a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel featuring a triplex microstructure of austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and TiB₂ (yellow). Panel (a) displays the EBSD-based representative volume element (RVE) measuring 200 μm × 200 μm, with periodic boundary conditions applied to the up/down/left/right edges (nodal sets a–d) and reference points RP-1 (loading) and RP-2 (fixed). Panel (b) depicts the 2D finite element model sliced along the thickness direction, highlighting phase boundaries including γ-Fe/TiB₂ PB and δ-Fe/TiB₂ PB. Panel (c) shows a close-up of the local finite-element meshes, where pixel-based automatic meshing assigns phases directly from EBSD image pixels using a 0.4 μm mesh size corresponding to 500 × 500 CPE4 elements. Caption states that the RVE utilizes plane-strain assumptions and periodic boundary conditions to approximate macroscopic mechanical responses, and this identical geometry was later employed to simulate both the present heterogeneous steel and a conventional Fe-TiB₂ reference, validating that the FEM framework reliably captures phase-specific stress partitioning and damage evolution.

Figure 3 benchmarks the mechanical and physical properties of the authors' low-density steel (LDS, Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) against reference steels and literature lightweight alloys. Panel (a) shows typical engineering stress-strain curves where 'Our LDS' (red) achieves markedly higher strength than Fe-TiB2 steel (orange) and interstitial-free (IF) steel (black); caption-reported values are σy = 503 MPa and σu = 672 MPa, which are 22% and 30% above the Fe-TiB2 reference (389 MPa and ~549 MPa), while the uniform elongation of 13% even exceeds the Fe-TiB2 steel's 11%. Panel (b) maps specific yield strength (SYS) versus uniform elongation (UE), placing the LDS (red star) in an optimal region among ceramic-reinforced and Al-added lightweight steels. Panel (c) plots the key lightweight parameter SYS × UE against specific modulus (E/ρ), showing the LDS (specific modulus 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³, SYS ~73.1 MPa g⁻¹ cm³) simultaneously achieves high stiffness and mechanical performance. The legend in panel (d) identifies comparative alloy families including Fe-Mn-Ti-B, Fe-Ti-B-(Nb), Fe-Cr-(Ni)-B-(Cu/C), Fe-(Zr/Hf/V/Nb/Ta/Mo/W)-B, and Fe-Mn-Al-C systems. Overall, the figure demonstrates that the present microstructural design delivers superior specific strength-ductility synergy without sacrificing specific modulus.

Figure 4 presents the initial microstructure and particle statistics of a hot-rolled Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B low-density steel. Panel (a) shows a pseudo-3D SEM view along RD, TD, and ND, revealing primary and eutectic TiB₂ particles distributed in the metallic matrix. Panel (b) is a high-magnification SEM image of the RD surface, displaying coarse, regularly shaped primary TiB₂ particles with hexagonal prism morphology. Panel (c) provides a high-magnification SEM image where dashed red lines delineate Mn-rich austenite and Al-rich δ-ferrite regions, corroborated by EDS elemental maps of Mn, Cr, and Al (inset). Panel (d) displays XRD patterns identifying three phases: δ-ferrite, austenite, and hexagonal TiB₂. Panels (e–g) present micro-CT results on TiB₂ particle distributions: (e) spatial maps in four size domains (0.8–5, 5–10, 10–20, and 20–42.7 μm), (f) number fractions showing small particles (0.8–5 μm) dominate by count, and (g) volume fractions showing coarse particles (10–42.7 μm) account for the majority of the total particle volume. Collectively, the data establish that the steel possesses a dual-phase ferrite-austenite matrix with a bimodal TiB₂ distribution where coarse primary particles govern the microstructural volume fraction despite fine eutectic debris prevailing in number.

Figure 5 presents comprehensive EBSD analysis of the as-processed Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel, comprising pseudo-3D stereographic views (a–a2) and 2D planar maps (b–b2, c–c2) together with quantitative histograms (b3, c3). Inverse pole figure maps (a, b) display grain orientations relative to the rolling (RD), transverse (TD), and normal (ND) directions. Phase maps (a1, b1) distinguish austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and TiB2 particles (yellow); caption reports area fractions of approximately 59%, 28%, and 13%, respectively, arranged in a banded matrix. Kernel average misorientation maps (a2, b2) reveal higher local misorientation (green–yellow) within δ-ferrite bands than in austenite, indicating greater dislocation accumulation in ferrite. The grain-size distribution (b3) shows austenite averaging 3.9 μm and δ-ferrite 4.4 μm. Grain orientation spread (GOS) maps (c, c1, c2) and the frequency plot (c3) demonstrate that 90.8% of austenite grains possess GOS < 2° (fully recrystallized), whereas only 52.2% of δ-ferrite grains do, indicating sluggish ferrite recrystallization during hot rolling. This figure establishes that the Al-stabilized δ-ferrite inherited from solidification forms deformed, elongated bands contrasting with recrystallized equiaxed austenite, creating the heterogeneous dual-phase matrix critical to the steel’s mechanical response.

This figure presents bright-field (BF) and weak-beam BF TEM micrographs characterizing dislocation morphologies in the hot-rolled Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B steel. Panels (a)-(a2) show delta-ferrite (BCC): (a) exhibits dense dislocation tangles within a grain distant from TiB2 particles, with a BCC <001> SADP inset; (a1) and (a2) reveal dislocation pile-ups and tangles at the delta-ferrite/TiB2 interface, with weak-beam imaging in (a2) at g = 101 (caption states) and a BCC <111> SADP. Panels (b)-(b2) show austenite (FCC): (b) displays sparse dislocations away from TiB2, with an FCC <011> SADP inset; (b1) and (b2) show pile-ups and sparser tangles at the austenite/TiB2 interface (marked by a blue line in b1), with weak-beam imaging in (b2) at g = 11\bar{1} (caption states). The comparison establishes that delta-ferrite harbors substantially higher dislocation density than austenite after hot rolling, and delta-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces accumulate denser dislocation networks than austenite/TiB2 interfaces. SADP evidence indicates a crystallographic orientation relationship at delta-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces consistent with coherent or semi-coherent boundaries, whereas austenite/TiB2 interfaces lack such a relationship. These findings confirm heterogeneous dislocation distribution and interface-dependent accumulation, supporting the role of grain-boundary-distributed TiB2 in the steel's initial deformation resistance.

This figure presents post-tensile characterization of a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel containing TiB₂, combining EBSD and TEM bright-field imaging. Panels (a–a₂) display pseudo-3D EBSD micrographs: (a) an IPF map showing grain orientations relative to RD, TD, and ND; (a₁) a phase map distinguishing BCC δ-ferrite, FCC austenite, and TiB₂ constituents; and (a₂) a KAM map revealing local misorientation distributions (50 μm scale). Panels (b–b₂) show TEM images of the BCC δ-ferrite phase: (b) a grain away from TiB₂ particles (per caption) with a BCC <110> zone-axis SADP; (b₁) dense dislocation pile-ups and tangles at a δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface; and (b₂) the corresponding SADP with g = 011. Panels (c–c₂) show the FCC austenite phase: (c) a grain away from TiB₂ (per caption) with an FCC <001> zone-axis SADP; (c₁) dislocation pile-ups with comparatively sparse tangles at an austenite/TiB₂ interface; and (c₂) the corresponding SADP with g = 111 (200 nm scale). The micrographs demonstrate significantly higher dislocation accumulation at δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interfaces than at austenite/TiB₂ interfaces, supporting a synergistic deformation mechanism where δ-ferrite provides a stable strengthening framework and austenite contributes increasing work hardening.

Figure 8 presents multi-scale SEM, EDS, and quantitative damage analysis of tensile failure in Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B low-density steel containing austenite (FCC), δ-ferrite (BCC), and hypereutectic TiB2. Pseudo-in-situ SEM images (a–a3) at 0%, 0.7%, 5%, and 9% engineering strain show progressive particle cracking (red arrows) and particle/matrix decohesion (blue arrows), with damage accumulating preferentially around FCC austenite. Quantified damage charts (a4, a5) reveal that both TiB2 crack and decohesion numbers increase with strain, exhibiting higher frequencies in the FCC phase than in the BCC phase. Cross-sectional views near the fracture surface (b–b2) display large primary TiB2 particles fractured perpendicular to the loading direction (blue arrows) and substantial interface debonding (red arrows), alongside cluster damage at high magnification. Fractography (c, c1) confirms a mixed mode of ductile matrix dimples, quasi-cleavage TiB2 fracture, and interfacial decohesion, with EDS elemental maps (Fe, Mn, Al, Ti, B) supporting the phase identification. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that stress-driven fracture of coarse primary TiB2 particles and progressive particle/matrix decohesion govern the damage evolution, producing a heterogeneous mixed ductile-brittle fracture surface.

Figure 9 presents local von Mises strain distributions obtained by correlative microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC) during pseudo-in-situ tensile testing of a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel. Panel (a) shows the initial EBSD phase map comprising austenite (γ), δ-ferrite (δ), and TiB₂ particles with the tensile axis parallel to the rolling direction (RD). Panels (b)–(f) display local strain maps at applied macroscopic strains of 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, and 15%, using a color scale from 0.00 to 0.20, revealing that strain localizations develop preferentially along directions approximately 45° to the loading axis and intensify with progressive deformation. Panels (g)–(i) provide corresponding line profiles extracted along the arrows in (a): (g) across small TiB₂/δ-ferrite boundaries showing ~0.10 local strain; (h) near TiB₂ particles of varying sizes in the matrix with elevated strain (~0.16) at austenite/TiB₂ interfaces; and (i) across sharp TiB₂/γ, TiB₂/δ, and γ/δ boundaries where strain concentrations reach ~0.20. These results demonstrate pronounced strain partitioning at ceramic/metal and soft/hard phase interfaces, indicating that TiB₂ particles effectively promote plastic deformation in the adjacent matrix and that the softer austenite phase accumulates higher local strain than δ-ferrite due to phase incompatibility, underpinning the enhanced strength-ductility balance.

This Figure presents synchrotron X-ray diffraction (XRD) line profiles and Modified Williamson–Hall (MWH) analyses for a low-density steel containing TiB2, δ-ferrite, and austenite. Subfigures (a) and (b) display the diffraction patterns plotted against reciprocal lattice spacing K (nm−1) for the undeformed sample and the sample after tensile straining to fracture, respectively, with peaks indexed to δ-ferrite (black), austenite (blue), and TiB2 (red). Subfigures (c) and (d) show the MWH plots of ΔK versus KC1/2 for δ-ferrite and austenite, comparing undeformed (solid squares) and deformed (open circles) conditions. Subfigure (e) summarizes the calculated dislocation densities for both phases before and after straining. The caption states that the initial dislocation density of δ-ferrite is (1.1±0.2)×1015 m−2, surpassing austenite at (3±0.7)×1014 m−2; after deformation, austenite rises significantly to (2±0.3)×1015 m−2 while δ-ferrite increases modestly to (1.7±0.7)×1015 m−2. This differential evolution demonstrates that austenite undergoes extensive dislocation multiplication during plastic deformation, supporting the composite deformation mechanism and strengthening behavior in the triplex steel.

This figure presents finite element simulation results for a low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B, labeled "Our LDS") containing δ-ferrite, austenite, and TiB₂ phases, compared with conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel and interstitial-free (IF) steel. Panel (a) shows true stress versus true strain curves under damaged (hollow circles) and undamaged (solid lines) conditions, with experimental data (solid colored markers) closely matching simulations; "Our LDS" achieves the highest strength (~750 MPa), followed by Fe-TiB₂ steel (~610 MPa) and IF steel (~460 MPa). Panel (b) plots dislocation density evolution in the two matrix phases: δ-ferrite (red) starts higher (~11×10¹⁴ m⁻²) and saturates, while austenite (blue) starts lower (~3×10¹⁴ m⁻²) but rises rapidly, eventually exceeding δ-ferrite, consistent with experimental measurements (dots). Panel (c) illustrates stress partitioning among phases as functions of global true stress: TiB₂ (yellow) bears the highest stress (>1200 MPa), followed by δ-ferrite (red) and austenite (blue). At a global stress of ~490 MPa, the rate change indicates austenite yields first, while δ-ferrite remains elastic and carries more load. These results validate the constitutive model and demonstrate that the heterogeneous triplex microstructure enables superior strength through uneven stress distribution and sequential yielding.

This figure presents simulated microstructural evolutions and mechanical responses of TiB2-reinforced steels during tensile deformation. Panels (a) and (b) compare the spatial distributions of dislocation density increment (ρ−ρ0, color scale 2–23×10^14 m^-2) in conventional Fe–TiB2 steel and the present low-density steel (Our LDS), respectively, at global strains of ε=0.0%, 5.0%, and 11.0%. The maps reveal that the homogeneous matrix of conventional steel develops intense shear bands oriented approximately 45° to the loading axis, leaving nearly undeformed zones (red circles) where dislocation slip is suppressed. In contrast, the heterogeneous LDS matrix exhibits more uniformly distributed dislocation densities, as microscopic strain partitioning between soft austenite and hard δ-ferrite diminishes shear band prominence until later stages (ε=11%). Panel (c) shows the experimental and simulated true stress–true strain curves alongside phase-specific strengthening contributions weighted by volume fractions: austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and elastic load-bearing TiB2 (orange). The caption reports that δ-ferrite provides a stable ~170 MPa base strengthening, exceeding 30% of the overall yield strength, while the austenite contribution increases progressively with strain. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that microstructural heterogeneity stabilizes plastic flow via synergistic deformation between a strong δ-ferrite framework and dislocation-hardening austenite, enabling the superior strength–ductility balance of the LDS.

Figure 13 presents a comparative analysis of TiB₂ particle damage evolution in a low-density steel (Fe–15.5Mn–3.95Al–5Ti–2.15B) under tensile loading at 5 % and 7 % global engineering strain, coupling scanning electron microscopy (SEM), micro-digital image correlation (μ-DIC), and finite element (FE) simulations. At 5 % strain, subfigures (a)–(a₃) show experimental SEM images of Particle 1 and Particle 2 fracture, alongside corresponding equivalent strain maps from μ-DIC (a₁), plastic equivalent strain (PEEQ) maps from FE (a₂), and σ₁₁ stress histories versus global strain (a₃). At 7 % strain, subfigures (b)–(b₃) capture Particle 3 decohesion and Particle 4 fracture, with matching strain distributions (b₁), PEEQ distributions (b₂), and stress curves revealing debonding and two-stage fracture events (b₃). Subfigure (c) schematically summarizes the microstructure evolution, depicting TiB₂ particle cracking voids and interfacial debonding voids within the δ-ferrite and austenite matrix. The figure demonstrates that localized strain concentrations drive both particle fracture and interfacial decohesion, with agglomerated particles fracturing preferentially due to initial defects, and confirms that particle fracture accelerates final specimen failure more severely than decohesion, validating the FE model's predictive capability.
